# 6 Key Benefits of a Teletherapy Practice

![](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Jill-Daino-1-1-1-150x150.jpg)
![](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/karmen-smith-round-3-150x150.png)
![](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Jill-Daino-1-1-1-150x150.jpg)
![Key Benefits of a Teletherapy Practice](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Key-Benefits-of-a-Teletherapy-Practice-800x500.jpg)

The growing trend of telehealth and teletherapy gained significant momentum due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Today, it continues to provide multiple benefits for both clients and therapists. As a platform, Talkspace emphasizes the flexibility offered by [online therapy](https://www.talkspace.com/) and makes mental healthcare more accessible, affordable, and convenient than ever.

Teletherapy has proven results, too — in one [study](https://liebertpub.com/doi/abs/10.1089/tmj.2016.0114?journalCode=tmj), more than 80% of people found it to be as or more effective than in-person, traditional therapy. The same survey shows that 98% of respondents found it more convenient. In short, if you haven’t already considered adding teletherapy to your services, it might be time to take a closer look at its [effectiveness](https://www.talkspace.com/research).

Here, we’re examining the benefits of teletherapy to help you determine if this might be a good next step for your career. Read on to learn more about the benefits of telehealth therapy.

### Be a Talkspace Therapist

Work flexible hours as part of a supportive virtual community of care providers. Receive ongoing training and free CE credits.

## 1. Convenience and Accessibility

The rise of telehealth services, and more specifically, teletherapy sessions, has revolutionized the way mental healthcare is provided, making it more convenient and accessible for both clients and therapists.

From the patient perspective, one of the major benefits of telehealth therapy is that it removes many of the obstacles that could prevent someone from getting mental health care, including limited mobility, remote locations, or a lack of time and transportation.

For the therapist, offering therapy sessions online means mental health services can be offered to a broader range of clients who may not have been able to attend in-person therapy due to various challenges.

The increased accessibility of a teletherapy service allows people with diverse needs and circumstances to receive the support they need without compromising quality care. It enables therapists to reach more lives and make more of a difference. Some telehealth therapy benefits include:

Beyond just offering convenience and accessibility benefits for clients, another benefit of teletherapy is that, because clients are more likely to feel comfortable and relaxed in their environment, there’s a strong possibility for increased engagement and better therapeutic rapport.

“I always imagined myself traveling and/or moving out of state once I retired from 9 to 5. When I joined Talkspace in 2017, it allowed me to continue doing clinical work wherever I call home. I can be licensed in one state and conduct therapy sessions anywhere with Internet access. This technology has given me the life of my dreams.”

## 2. Increased Efficiency and Productivity

One of the most significant benefits of teletherapy sessions is increased efficiency and productivity. Therapists can see more clients in a day without feeling rushed or overwhelmed.

Technology is critical to streamlining administrative tasks. For instance, the online therapy platform, Talkspace, provides tools that simplify scheduling, billing, and note-taking processes for therapists on their platform.

![Become a Talkspace provider. Take your practice to the next level. Appy now!](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/600x300_Talkspace-CTA-v01.png)

## 3. Cost Savings to You and Your Clients

A teletherapy practice can significantly reduce costs for both therapists and their clients. For example, you can reduce overhead costs for maintaining a physical office space, such as rent and utilities. These cost savings can be passed on to your clients in various ways.

If you’re wondering [how to make more money as a therapist](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/how-to-make-money-as-a-therapist), this benefit should appeal to you because lower overhead means more money back to you.

## 4. Improved Outcomes

Teletherapy can offer improved outcomes through more effective treatment for various mental health issues. It provides a safe and comfortable environment that allows clients to access help from anywhere at any time. This modality can be particularly effective in specific [types of therapy](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/different-types-therapy-psychotherapy-best/), such as exposure therapy.

[Research shows](https://liebertpub.com/doi/abs/10.1089/tmj.2016.0114?journalCode=tmj) that teletherapy is just as effective as traditional in-person therapy. For example, in a [recent study](https://bmcpsychiatry.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12888-020-02721-x), 70% of participants reported significant improvement in their anxiety or depression symptoms after receiving messaging therapy — and 50% recovered from their symptoms entirely and no longer met the criteria for a depression or anxiety diagnosis.

## 5. Safe and Comfortable Environment

One of the key benefits of teletherapy sessions is that they provide clients with a safe and comfortable environment to discuss their concerns. Anyone who experiences apprehension or embarrassment about going to traditional therapy sessions, particularly those struggling with painful issues such as abuse or trauma, might find solace in teletherapy’s secure atmosphere.

It’s common for clients to find it easier to open up and share their thoughts and feelings when participating in teletherapy from the privacy of their own homes. The comfort generated by teletherapy can lead to more successful discussions in therapy, which may benefit the client’s overall results.

“When I worked in the child welfare setting, I would see clients in an environment where they weren’t comfortable sharing their emotional trauma. It was a setting where they might have felt judged and misunderstood; it certainly wasn’t a setting to be vulnerable. Online therapy can take place in any setting the client feels supported in their healing. The therapist is in their comfortable space, surrounded by what gives them peace. The setting and environment sets the stage for healing.”

Wondering [how to start an online therapy practice](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/how-to-start-online-therapy-practice)? Talkspace is here to help! Online platforms like [Talkspace](https://web.fountain.com/talkspace/apply/talkspace-practitioner-therapist-expedited-enrollment?utm_source=blog&utm_medium=article_link&utm_content=benefits-of-teletherapy) offer secure video conferencing tools explicitly designed for use within human services settings. These tools ensure client confidentiality during a virtual session while adhering to ethical obligations set forth by professional organizations such as the American Psychological Association ([APA](https://www.apa.org/practice/guidelines/telepsychology)) and the National Association of Social Workers ([NASW](https://www.socialworkers.org/includes/newIncludes/homepage/PRA-BRO-33617.TechStandards_FINAL_POSTING.pdf)).

## 6. Better Work-Life Balance

A teletherapy practice can provide therapists with improved [work-life balance](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/work-life-balance/), including increased schedule flexibility and less stress from daily commutes. It can help reduce strain and enhance health, resulting in a better balance between work and personal life.

Some key advantages that contribute to better work-life balance include:

“When I retired, I was not looking for another office job but was looking to find a way to continue clinical work. I wanted to work with a population that wanted to improve the quality of their life by increasing their self-awareness. Online therapy provides an opportunity for self-discovery.”

## Experience the Benefits of an Online Practice with Talkspace

As a mental health professional and licensed therapist, embracing teletherapy can significantly enhance your practice and benefit you and your clients. By joining Talkspace, you can experience these advantages firsthand while leveraging the platform’s unique features.

By joining the [Talkspace network](https://web.fountain.com/talkspace/apply/talkspace-practitioner-therapist-expedited-enrollment?utm_source=blog&utm_medium=article_link&utm_content=benefits-of-teletherapy) you’ll experience all these benefits of teletherapy and more while growing your teletherapy practice. Discover our top [teletherapy tips](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/teletherapy-tips-for-therapists) and best practices for therapists. We can help you develop your professional career and make a meaningful difference in the lives of many through online counseling.

***Sources:***

Talkspace articles are written by experienced [mental health-wellness contributors](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/talkspace-council-of-experts-meet/); they are grounded in scientific research and evidence-based practices. Articles are extensively reviewed by our team of clinical experts (therapists and psychiatrists of various specialties) to ensure content is accurate and on par with current industry standards.

Our goal at Talkspace is to provide the most up-to-date, valuable, and objective information on mental health-related topics in order to help readers make informed decisions.

Articles contain trusted third-party sources that are either directly linked to in the text or listed at the bottom to take readers directly to the source.

![](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/karmen-smith-round-3-150x150.png)

##### [Karmen Smith LCSW, DD](https://www.talkspace.com/blog/author/karmensmith/)

Licensed Talkspace Therapist, Dr. Karmen Smith LCSW, CSIM is a Clinical Social Worker with over 30 years of experience who has worked to reunite parents with their children who have been in foster care. Most families have experienced generational trauma and are not aware of the patterns they were perpetuating. Dr. Smith has given workshops and classes on understanding the trauma triggers so that foster parents, parole officers and shelter staff can be empathetic caregivers, which stops the cycle of abuse. Dr. Smith developed a psychological model of identifying the energetic motion of dysfunctional behavior called the “Human Hamster Wheel” which provides a visual representation for therapeutic purposes.

# News

# Benefits of Telehealth Visits for Mental Health Patients

As telemedicine proves to be an increasing method of clinical care, a recent [JAMA study](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama-health-forum/fullarticle/2811062) shows it could be more essential to preventative and follow-up care than previously understood.

Authored by Research Associate Andrew Wilcock, PhD, Professor of Health Care Policy Haiden Huskamp, PhD, Associate Professor of Health Care Policy Alisa Busch, PhD, Professor of Health Care Policy Sharon-Lise Normand, PhD, Professor of Health Care Policy Jose Zubizarreta, PhD, Professor of Health Care Policy Ateev Mehrotra, MD, MPH and co-authors, the study investigates the impact of telemedicine on care quality for patients with serious mental illness (SMI), such as schizophrenia and bipolar 1 disorder.

The study evaluated changes in mental health visits, drug adherence, hospital use, post-discharge follow-up visits, and patient mortality for SMI patients cared for by practices with higher telemedicine use during the first year of the pandemic compared with practices that relied mostly on in-person care.

The study showed that patients at practices with the highest telemedicine use had more mental health visits and better continuity of care. And while the increase in visits did not impact medication adherence or reductions in hospitalizations, the study suggests that telemedicine can be an effective way to improve patient-clinician engagement and continuity of care in a vulnerable patient population.

###### [Online Therapy](/us/basics/online-therapy)

# 14 Benefits of Teletherapy for Clients

## Online counseling is the new frontier. Reasons to consider taking the plunge.

Posted January 28, 2019

|
[Reviewed by Jessica Schrader](/us/docs/editorial-process)

![Share on Facebook](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-facebook-md.svg)
![Share on X](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-twitter-md.svg)
![Share on Bluesky](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-bluesky-blue.svg)
![Share on LinkedIn](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-linkedin-md.svg)
![Share via Email](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-email-md.svg)
![Adobe Stock Images](https://cdn.psychologytoday.com/sites/default/files/styles/image-article_inline_full_caption/public/field_blog_entry_images/2019-01/240_f_108778346_pfm6hayf0kefb0guqwihdzxayukjjrcz-1_0.jpg?itok=8HqHXfuE "Adobe Stock Images")

*\*In the first of this two-part series, I will be exploring the benefits of teletherapy for clients. In Part 2, I will explore therapist-specific benefits of teletherapy.*

In our digital revolution, [innovation](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/creativity "Psychology Today looks at innovation") is ever rapidly changing our daily lives. Adapting, whether as digital immigrants or natives, is required to survive anymore. Why then, do we keep revisiting antiquated ways of doing things? As therapists, we know that much has changed in the landscape of [psychotherapy](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/therapy "Psychology Today looks at psychotherapy"). What was once therapy sessions generously covered under insurance benefits during the Golden Age of Therapy 25 years ago ([Grodzki, 2015](https://www.amazon.com/Building-Your-Ideal-Private-Practice/dp/0393703312)), has been hacked away by modern HMO companies requiring endless streams of codes and paperwork. Further, wait times in rural and large cities for therapy services increase as coverage decreases. Online therapy options are the new frontier. One that many may resist, but that ultimately provide major benefits to clients and therapists alike.

Having recently moved to a fully [virtual therapy](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/online-therapy "Psychology Today looks at virtual therapy") practice using live video (e.g., like FaceTime but using HIPAA-compliant technology), the benefits have been immediate and in many ways unexpected. It is no surprise that telehealth is currently a $6 billion industry with estimates that it will reach close to $20 billion in 2025 ([HCInnovation, 2018](https://www.hcinnovationgroup.com/clinical-it/news/13030029/report-telehealth-market-estimated-to-reach-195b-by-2025)). While I initially dabbled with teletherapy more by accident than intention (starting with college students in-state living two to three hours away from my office), I quickly came to see its benefits for a much larger range of clients. Below, are listed some of the most notable benefits to date.

While there is no doubt that much more needs to be done before clients and therapists fully embrace and explore online therapy options, the future certainly looks bright. With more and more individuals telecommuting, busier schedules, and the need for greater flexibility in our ever-changing world, teletherapy opens up a whole new frontier. For more on therapist benefits of teletherapy, click [here](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/millennial-media/201901/13-benefits-teletherapy-therapists).

# 7 Benefits of Teletherapy for Therapists and Patients

Polling from the [Kaiser Family Foundation](https://www.kff.org/health-reform/press-release/brief-examines-the-covid-19-crisis-implications-for-americans-mental-health/) found that nearly half of adults in America say that “worry and stress related to the coronavirus (COVID-19) pandemic are hurting their mental health.” With shelter-in-place orders enforced, most in-person therapy was discontinued, reducing mental health support options for those who needed it most.

Given this situation, the potential of teletherapy to help people get the mental healthcare they need is of particular importance today. Here are seven benefits:

Teletherapy has come a long way in recent years, especially given advancements in video-conferencing technologies and increased cybersecurity. The pandemic has further driven teletherapy to the forefront of mental healthcare, resulting in changes in regulations and law surrounding teletherapy and health insurance requirements. To remain relevant in these uncertain times , therapists will want to stay current and educate themselves in the many benefits and effective applications of modern teletherapy.

Lamar University’s online Master of Education (M.Ed.) in Clinical Mental Health Counseling program offers students a unique, hybrid educational experience while they earn a degree in this growing field. Students strengthen the knowledge and skills they gain through online coursework by completing seminars incorporating face-to-face individual and group therapy online as well as practicums and internships.

Although this study has clear application to traditional, in-person therapy, the direct experience with online therapy modalities prepares students to be effective teletherapy practitioners.

*Learn more about* [*Lamar University’s online Master of Education (M.Ed.) in Clinical Mental Health Counseling program*](/online-programs/education/med-counseling/clinical-mental-health/)*.*

enriched_points.expanded_content: Teletherapy eliminates travel time, allowing clients to attend sessions from home, enhancing accessibility and making mental health care easier to integrate into daily life.
Clients often feel more comfortable discussing sensitive issues in familiar environments, leading to higher levels of participation and openness during remote therapy sessions.
Studies indicate that teletherapy supports better continuity of care, leading to improved medication adherence and follow-up appointments, especially in vulnerable populations.
The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the adoption of teletherapy, providing essential mental health services when in-person visits were limited, ensuring ongoing support for many individuals.
Despite its benefits, teletherapy can create barriers to personal connection, as non-verbal cues may be harder to interpret, potentially impacting the therapeutic relationship.
